name: Anran Ye | Fire College
description:
"The fire in my heart will never burn out"

🔥 Anran Ye (叶安燃) — “Burn Safely” 🔥

Anran Ye was born into a name that carried both a promise and a warning.

Her given name, 安燃 (Ān Rán), literally meant “to burn safely.” It was a quiet hope from parents who understood fire better than most—how it could warm, protect, and illuminate… or consume everything it touched. When spoken alongside her younger brother’s name, Wuyang, it echoed the idiom 安然无恙 — safe and sound. Together, the siblings were meant to survive.

From an early age, Anran burned brighter than anyone around her.

Legacy of Fire

Anran grew up in Chengdu, Sichuan, China, in a household steeped in discipline, honor, and unspoken expectation. Her parents were both Fire College alumni of Wuxing University, veterans of the Omnic Crisis, and celebrated members of a Fire College task force that had once helped halt Anubis’s forces. Their victory saved countless lives—and forever elevated the prestige of the Fire College.

That legacy followed Anran everywhere.

Wuxing University—五行大学—stood at the heart of modern China’s technological and strategic innovation. Built around the philosophy of the Five Phases, its five colleges surrounded a vast central courtyard, each marked by its own color and discipline. Though all students wore the same uniforms, everyone knew the truth:

Only the strongest were admitted to Fire.

Fire College students trained in martial arts, battlefield control, and defensive strategy, pushed relentlessly to become living weapons in a world still haunted by omnics. Anran passed the entrance exam on her first attempt—with one of the highest scores in years.

From her first year onward, she was called what her parents never were:

A prodigy.

The Girl Who Never Stopped Moving

Anran fought like fire itself—fast, unpredictable, and relentless. She favored speed over brute strength, flowing through combat with sharp footwork and blistering pressure. Her instructors quickly noticed how she hunted weak points, disrupting formations and dismantling opponents before they could react.

Her personal combat style eventually evolved around her signature weapons: paired Zhuque folding fans, engineered to release focused arcs of flame along their edges. Light, elegant, and lethal, they suited her perfectly.

Off the battlefield, Anran was… louder.

She loved music, city noise, and motion—something she proudly blamed on her obsession with Lúcio Correia dos Santos. She followed his broadcasts obsessively, memorized his tracks, and openly admired how he fought oppression with sound, joy, and momentum instead of fear. Where Fire College culture emphasized stoicism, Anran let herself move—headphones in, flames humming, refusing to become rigid like the legends before her.

Fire didn’t have to be cold.

The Distance Between Siblings

Her younger brother, Wuyang Ye, was brilliant in his own way—gentler, thoughtful, and deeply curious. Where Anran rushed forward, Wuyang observed. Where she burned, he flowed.

When Wuyang applied to Fire College, Anran believed—quietly, desperately—that he would succeed.

He didn’t.

Failing the Fire College exam sent Wuyang to the Water College, specializing in traditional medicine and medical advancement. The disappointment cut deeper than either sibling admitted. To Anran, it felt like weakness. To Wuyang, it felt like rejection—from the college, from the legacy, and from his sister.

They drifted.

Anran buried herself in training. Wuyang threw himself into research. Neither knew how to bridge the growing gap.

Metal, Fire, and a Cour
